notes: Imperative form of 'regarder'. Used constantly with children. Tu form (no -s in imperative for -er verbs).

example_sentence: Oh non, il est tombé par terre !
example_translation: Oh no, he fell on the ground!
notes: Passé composé with être. 'Elle est tombée' for feminine — the past participle agrees.

example_sentence: Où est-elle ? Elle se cache derrière le canapé.
example_translation: Where is she? She's hiding behind the sofa.
notes: Reflexive verb 'se cacher'. Spoken: 'elle s'cache'.

example_sentence: Attention, c'est chaud ! Ne touche pas.
notes: 'Attention' works on its own as 'watch out!' too.

example_translation: Come here, we're going to eat.
notes: Irregular imperative of 'venir'. 'Venez ici' for vous form.

example_sentence: Allez, on y va ! On est en retard.
example_translation: Come on, let's go! We're late.
notes: Extremely common. 'On y va ?' as a question means 'shall we go?'

example_sentence: C'est bien, tu as fini ton assiette !
example_translation: Well done, you finished your plate!
notes: Also 'c'est très bien' for emphasis.
Imperative of 'arrêter'. 'Arrêtez' for vous form.
Very versatile: can mean gently, slowly, quietly, or easy depending on context.
Used until late afternoon. 'Bonsoir' from evening onward.
Informal. Also works as a statement: 'ça va' = 'I'm fine'. Spoken: often just 'ça va' with rising intonation.

example_sentence: Il ne vient pas. — Ah bon ? Pourquoi ?
example_translation: He's not coming. — Oh really? Why?
notes: Essential reactive phrase. Rising intonation = surprise. Flat intonation = mild acknowledgment.
More casual alternatives: 'à plus' (spoken: 'à plus'), 'à tout à l'heure' (see you later today).
'Bonne soirée' for evening. Very commonly said when parting.
Slightly more complete than 'ça va ?' but still informal. Formal: 'comment allez-vous ?'

example_sentence: Ça va ? — Pas mal, et toi ?
example_translation: How are you? — Not bad, and you?
notes: Common casual response. Also means 'quite a lot' in other contexts: 'pas mal de monde' = quite a lot of people.
Informal. 'Et vous ?' for formal/plural.

example_translation: Thank you very much, that's very kind.

translation: oh my / oh wow / oh dear
notes: NOT a cliché — genuinely used constantly. Expresses surprise, frustration, admiration, or exasperation depending on tone.

example_translation: Hold on, I'm looking for my keys.
notes: Imperative of 'attendre'. 'Attendez' for formal. Spoken: often 'attends attends attends' repeated for emphasis.

example_sentence: Tu pleures ? Qu'est-ce qui s'est passé ?
notes: Learn as a whole chunk. Spoken: often contracted to 'qu'est-ce qui s'passe ?' (present) or 'qu'est-ce qui s'est passé ?' (past).

translation: no way / I can't believe it
example_sentence: Il a dit ça ? C'est pas vrai !
notes: Spoken form drops 'ne': 'c'est pas vrai' not 'ce n'est pas vrai'. Written form keeps 'ne'.

example_sentence: Le magasin est fermé. Tant pis, on reviendra demain.
example_translation: The store is closed. Too bad, we'll come back tomorrow.
notes: Opposite: 'tant mieux' = so much the better / great.

translation: it's not a big deal / no worries
notes: Spoken drops 'ne'. Written: 'ce n'est pas grave'. Very common reassurance.

example_translation: Are you coming tomorrow? — Of course!
notes: 'Bien sûr que oui' for emphasis. 'Bien sûr que non' = of course not.

example_sentence: On se retrouve à midi ? — D'accord.
notes: Shortened to 'd'ac' in very casual speech/texting.

translation: there you go / here it is / that's it
notes: Incredibly versatile. Can mean 'here it is', 'there you go', 'that's it', 'exactly', or be used as a filler/conclusion marker.
Two meanings: (1) 'finally' and (2) as a filler meaning 'well' or 'I mean' (correcting yourself). Context tells you which.

example_sentence: Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.
example_translation: I would like a coffee, please.
notes: Conditional of 'vouloir' — learn as a chunk, grammar comes later. More polite than 'je veux'.
'S'il te plaît' for informal (tu). Abbreviated 'svp' in writing.
Feminine: 'l'addition'. At a restaurant, you must ask for it — it won't come automatically.

example_translation: These tomatoes, how much do they cost?
notes: More formal: 'combien est-ce que ça coûte ?' or 'quel est le prix ?'. Spoken: 'c'est combien ?' is even more casual.
Uses 'avoir' not 'être'. 'De' contracts to 'd'' before a vowel. Anglophones often try to say 'je besoin' — always 'j'ai besoin de'.

example_translation: I'm hungry, are we eating soon?
notes: French uses 'avoir' (to have) not 'être' (to be): 'I have hunger' not 'I am hungry'. Common anglophone error.

example_sentence: J'ai soif, il y a de l'eau ?
example_translation: I'm thirsty, is there any water?
notes: Same pattern as 'j'ai faim' — uses 'avoir'. 'J'ai très soif' = I'm very thirsty.

example_sentence: Où est la gare, s'il vous plaît ?
example_translation: Where is the train station, please?
notes: 'Où sont... ?' for plural. Spoken: 'il est où ?' inverts the structure informally.
The easiest way to form questions — just put 'est-ce que' at the start. Spoken: intonation alone also works ('tu parles anglais ?').
No preposition needed — 'chercher' directly takes an object. Anglophones often add 'pour' by mistake.

example_sentence: Il y a un chat dans le jardin.
example_translation: There is a cat in the garden.
notes: Spoken form: 'y'a' — teach both. Works for singular and plural. 'Il n'y a pas de...' for negative (spoken: 'y'a pas de...').

translation: it is / this is / that is
notes: Used far more than 'il est' / 'elle est' in spoken French. 'C'est' + adjective for general descriptions, 'il/elle est' + adjective for specific people/things.

example_sentence: Où est le livre ? — Je sais pas.
example_translation: Where is the book? — I don't know.
notes: Spoken form drops 'ne': 'je sais pas' not 'je ne sais pas'. Spoken: often 'chais pas' or even 'ch'pas'. Written: always 'je ne sais pas'.
Literally 'I call myself'. Reflexive verb 's'appeler'. 'Comment tu t'appelles ?' = What's your name? (informal).

example_translation: My name is Pierre. — Nice to meet you!
notes: Add -e if you're female: 'enchantée'. In casual contexts, 'salut' + name is enough.
